claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 12 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority; Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'm asking about - Alex, who's 12, and Blake, who's 6.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have two children - Alex (age 12) and Blake (age 6).

Now I need to ask: **Do Alex and Blake currently live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes. Alex doesn't live with me though - they live elsewhere. Blake does live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Blake lives with you in the UK, which is good for Blake's eligibility.

Regarding Alex, who doesn't live with you - I need to ask: **Do you contribute financially to Alex's upkeep (such as paying for food, clothes, pocket money, or contributing to their accommodation)?** If yes, approximately how much do you contribute per week or month?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with Alex. I contribute towards things like clothes and bits and pieces they need - it's about £20 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Contributing £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep is helpful to know.

To better assess your situation with Alex, I need to ask: **How long has it been since Alex stopped living with you?** Has it been more or less than 8 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been quite a bit longer than that - it's been several months now since Alex moved out.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that it's been several months since Alex moved out.

The £20 per week you're contributing is important here. For reference, the current Child Benefit rates are:
- £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child
- £16.95 per week for each additional child

Since Alex is your eldest child, you would need to be contributing at least £25.60 per week (or the equivalent value) towards Alex's upkeep to continue receiving Child Benefit for them beyond the initial 8 weeks.

Now, a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, actually - for Blake, their other parent claims Child Benefit and Blake lives with them as well.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I need to clarify something important here. You mentioned earlier that Blake lives with you, but now you're saying Blake lives with their other parent who is claiming Child Benefit. 

**Can you help me understand Blake's living situation? Does Blake:**
- Live with you full-time?
- Live with the other parent full-time?
- Split time between both households?

And to confirm - is the other parent currently receiving Child Benefit for Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I'm sorry for the confusion. Blake does live with me, but the other parent also lives in the household with us. They're the one who claims the Child Benefit for Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clearing that up! So you all live together in the same household, and Blake's other parent is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

That makes sense. Just to complete the picture for Alex - **is anyone else (perhaps whoever Alex is living with) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just a couple more questions to complete my assessment:

1. **Are you currently receiving Child Benefit payments for Alex?** (Have you been claiming it since before Alex moved out?)

2. **Regarding your £20 per week contribution to Alex - could you increase this to at least match the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week, or would you be able to provide additional support "in kind" (like providing accommodation, meals when visiting, etc.) to bring the total value up to that amount?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, I'm not currently receiving Child Benefit for Alex - I'm inquiring now to see if I can claim it.

As for increasing the amount, I'm contributing what I can at the moment, which is the £20 per week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that you're not currently receiving Child Benefit for either child and are inquiring about whether you can claim it now.

Let me make sure I have all the information I need:

**Final question: Is there anyone else in Alex's life who might also be contributing to their upkeep, or are you the only person providing financial support to Alex apart from whoever they're living with?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm the only one providing financial support to Alex, yes.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake under your current circumstances. Here's why:

**For Blake:**
Blake lives with you, which would normally be a good basis for claiming Child Benefit. However, Blake's other parent (who also lives in your household) is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. According to the rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child - two people cannot claim for the same child. Since the other parent is already receiving it, you cannot also claim it for Blake.

**For Alex:**
Alex doesn't live with you, which means to be considered "responsible" for them and qualify for Child Benefit, you would need to contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards their upkeep. For an eldest child, the Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week. You're currently contributing £20 per week, which is below this threshold. To be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, you would need to increase your contributions to at least £25.60 per week (or provide equivalent support "in kind" such as accommodation, meals, etc.).

**What you could consider:**
- If you could increase your financial support to Alex to at least £25.60 per week (or equivalent value), you may become eligible to claim for Alex
- For Blake, you and the other parent would need to agree for the current claim to stop before you could make a new claim

I hope this explanation is helpful. Please let me know if you have any questions!

reasonings: Alex is 12 years old and does not live with the claimant. According to the rules, you'll usually get Child Benefit for 8 weeks after your child goes to live with someone else if nobody else claims. However, the claimant is not currently receiving Child Benefit for Alex and this is a new claim inquiry. To qualify for Child Benefit when a child lives elsewhere, the claimant must be responsible for the child by contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep. For the eldest child, the Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week. The claimant is contributing £20 per week, which is below the required threshold of £25.60 per week. Additionally, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, but the claimant's contributions are insufficient to establish responsibility under the rules. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 6 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. However, Blake's other parent also lives in the same household and is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake. According to the rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child. Two people cannot get Child Benefit for the same child. Since the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, the claimant cannot also claim Child Benefit for Blake. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (12) and Blake (6). For Blake, the claimant lives with the child but Blake's other parent (who also lives in the household) is already claiming Child Benefit, and only one person can claim per child. For Alex, who lives elsewhere, the claimant contributes £20 per week but this falls short of the £25.60 per week Child Benefit rate required to establish responsibility for a child living away from home. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child under current circumstances.
